Keywords: TFM, FRET-based tension probes, VinTS, focal adhesions, mechanobiology, cellular forces

Abstract

Focal adhesions (FAs) are mechanosensitive structures that mediate force transmission between cells and  the  extracellular  matrix.  While  Traction  Force  Microscopy  (TFM)  quantifies  cellular  tractions exerted on deformable substrates, Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET)-based tension probes, such as Vinculin Tension Sensors (VinTS), measure molecular-scale forces within FA proteins. Despite their potential synergy, these methods have rarely been combined to explore the interplay between molecular tension and cellular tractions. Here, we introduce a framework integrating TFM and VinTS to investigate  FA  mechanics  across  scales.  At  cell  level,  tractions  and  vinculin  tension  increased  with substrate stiffness. At FA level, vinculin tension correlated with vinculin density, while tractions scaled with  FA  area,  total  vinculin  content  and  vinculin  density.  Direct  comparison  of  tractions  to  tension revealed a complex, heterogenous relationship between these forces, possibly linked to diverse cell and  FA  maturation  states.  Sub-FA  analysis  revealed  conserved  spatial  patterns,  with  tension  and traction  increasing  towards  the  cell  periphery.  This  mulPscale  approach  provides  insights  into  the mulPscale  dynamics  of  FA  mechanotransducPon,  bridging  the  gap  between  molecular  forces  and cellular mechanics.j

Introduc3on

Mechanobiology  is  an  interdisciplinary  field  that  invesPgates  the  reciprocal  relaPonship  between mechanical forces and biological processes. MechanotransducPon - the process by which cells sense and respond to mechanical signals from their environment - plays a crucial role in fundamental cellular funcPons such as proliferaPon, differenPaPon and migraPon, as well as in pathological condiPons like fibrosis, cancer and cardiovascular diseases 1-5 .  Deeper understanding of mechanotransducPon offers insights into cellular behavior and disease mechanisms, leading to innovaPve therapies that restore the mechanical equilibrium in the cellular microenvironment.

Central to mechanotransducPon are focal adhesions (FAs), mulPfuncPonal complexes at the plasmamembrane  that  physically  connect  the  acPn  cytoskeleton  to  the  extracellular  matrix  (ECM).  These structures are pivotal for sensing mechanical cues and translaPng them into cellular responses. Through actomyosin-mediated contracPlity, FAs transmit forces from the cytoskeleton to the ECM, allowing cells to sense their surroundings and convert biophysical cues into cellular processes such as cell migraPon, differenPaPon, cell-cycle progression and cell death 6,7 .  FAs  exhibit  a  complex structure and dynamic protein  composiPon,  undergoing  changes  in  both  morphology  and  composiPon  based  on  their maturaPon  stage 8,9 . Vinculin,  a  mechanosensiPve  protein,  is  essenPal  for  FA  maturaPon  and strengthening.  It  transiPons  from  an  auto-inhibited  state  to  an  acPve  form  through  ligand-assisted unfolding and stretching 10-13 .  Once acPvated, vinculin interacts with other FA proteins such as acPn, paxillin and talin to mediate mechanosensing and lamellipodial acPn dynamics 14-17 . These roles make vinculin is a key player in cell adhesion, migraPon, and mechanotransducPon 18,19 .

One of the most widely used methods to quanPfy forces transmiRed by FAs at the cell-ECM interface is TracPon Force Microscopy (TFM). TradiPonally applied to 2D in vitro cultures, TFM involves seeding the cells on a synthePc, ECM-like substrate 20-22 . The substrate, typically an elasPc hydrogel with embedded fiducial markers, is imaged using opPcal microscopy to capture two states: a stressed state, where cells are  mechanically  acPve,  and  stress-free  or  relaxed  state,  where cells  have  either  been  removed  or actomyosin  contracPlity  has  been  disrupted.  Image  processing  algorithms  track  the  movement  of fiducial markers between these states to measure hydrogel deformaPon. Using the known mechanical properPes of the hydrogel, mechanical models can infer cell-generated forces and tracPons (force per unit of area) from these displacements. Unlike canPlever or micropillar-based TFM 23,24 , hydrogel-based TFM provides a conPnuous substrate for cellular adhesion, allowing cells to developed FA morphologies that are not restricted by a certain cross-secPonal area. This approach also allows for the computaPon of discrePzed tracPon fields, theorePcally limited only by the opPcal resoluPon of the microscope.

While TFM is instrumental in quanPfying and visualizing the tracPons transmiRed via FAs, it does not provide a direct readout of the molecular tension within FA proteins, as it captures tracPons exerted at the substrate level. Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET)-based molecular tension probes address this limitaPon by directly measuring forces at the molecular scale 25 . These probes detect conformaPonal changes by measuring the changes in FRET - a phenomenon where energy transfers non-radiaPvely between two nearby fluorophores - in response to mechanical force 26 .  FRET-based  tension  probes consist of a pair of fluorescent proteins ( Donor and Acceptor ) linked by a short, flexible polypepPde that undergoes conformaPonal changes upon tensional forces. When no tension is applied, the fluorescent proteins remain in close proximity and show high-FRET efficiency. When the linker is stretched under tension, the distance between the fluorescent proteins increases, leading to a decrease in FRET. This force-induced separaPon of the FRET-pair and subsequent change in FRET allows for sensiPve detecPon of  molecular  forces  in  the  pN  range,  making  FRET-based  tension  probes  powerful  for  studying mechanobiology at the molecular scale. Among the FRET-based tension probes, Vinculin Tension Sensor (VinTS) is the best characterized and has been extensively used to invesPgate FA mechanics 9,24,25,27-33 . By  incorporaPng  the  FRET  tension  probes  within  vinculin,  VinTS  reveals  the  magnitude  and  spaPal distribuPon  of  forces  within  FAs,  offering  a  molecular  view  on  force  transmission  at  cell-matrix interfaces.

While TFM and FRET-based tension probes are powerful techniques on their own, they offer insights into cell-matrix forces at different levels. TFM quanPfies relaPvely large forces that are sufficient to induce measurable substrate deformaPons, depending on factors like substrate sPffness. These forces originate from subcellular regions, such as FAs and lamellipodia. In contrast, FRET-based tension probes report the molecular forces at the nanoscale. To date, only two studies have combined FRET tension probes with cell-ECM force measurements. Chang et al. (2013) performed TFM on cells expressing VinTS and reported a slight inverse linear correlaPon between mean tracPon and mean FRET raPo at FAs. However, their  analysis  was  limited  by  the  low-throughput  of  just  four  cells 27 .  Sarangi  et  al.  (2017)

This approach revealed  how  changes  in  tracPon  forces  corresponded  to  variaPons  in  vinculin  tension 24 . However, their in vitro system confined FAs to the top of micropillars, which may not accurately reflect naPve FA behavior and could influence their dynamics or funcPonality.

In this study, we established an experimental and computaPonal framework integraPng hydrogel-based TFM, which enables FA formaPon on an unrestricPve, conPnuous substrate, with VinTS to invesPgate the interplay between vinculin tension and tracPon forces at FAs. Our findings showed that tracPons increase with FA area, vinculin content and density, while vinculin tension was mainly regulated by vinculin density. At cell-level, we observed both tension and tracPon magnitude increase with increasing substrate sPffness. At FA-level, we idenPfied three correlaPon paRerns between vinculin tension and tracPon - direct, inverse and uncorrelated - suggesPng varied cellular and FA states. At sub-FA level, vinculin tension and tracPon followed a similar trend along the longitudinal axis of FAs, consistent across different  cell  and  FA  populaPons.  The  framework  advances  our  understanding  of  subcellular mechanotransducPon and provides a robust foundaPon for future studies of cellular force dynamics and mechanobiology.

Results

Integrated Workflow for TFM and FRET-based tension probes

To integrate TFM with FRET-based tension probes, we developed a custom protocol to overcome the technical challenges of combining these methods (Fig. 1a, details in the methods secPon). Sub-cellular FRET imaging requires high numerical aperture (NA) objecPves, which have a limited working distance of  ~300  μm.  To  ensure  that  cells  on  the  polyacrylamide  (PAA)  gel  remained  within  the  objecPve's working distance, we fabricated thin, defect-free PAA substrates using 250 μm thick silicone spacers and #0 coverslips. The spacers also created flat gel surfaces, aligning all focal adhesions within the same zplane, thereby facilitaPng FRET imaging. To calculate tracPons exerted by individual mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs) accurately, we maintained a low cell density of ~25 cells/mm 2 .  At this density, cell spreading required opPmal collagen coaPng, which was achieved by using an acidic soluPon 34 . VinTStransfected vinculin double knock out MEFs are seeded on the collagen-coated gels and allowed to spread overnight before imaging.

Similar  to  sample  preparaPon, combining TFM and FRET-probes required a specific data acquisiPon protocol (Fig. 1b). Due to the dynamic behavior of fibroblasts, which induces relaPvely fast changes in cell morphology, it is crucial to minimize the Pme interval between the TFM and FRET data acquisiPon. First,  bead  images  of  the  stressed  state  were  acquired,  followed  by  Fluorescence  LifePme  Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) images of the donor channel (used to calculate FRET efficiency) 35-38 . To analyze the vinculin content and the morphology of the FAs, fluorescence image of the acceptor channel was then acquired. These steps were repeated for ~5-10 cells in the sample. Next, cells were removed using sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), allowing the relaxaPon of the PAA gel, and bead images of the relaxed state were acquired for all measured cells, complePng the data acquisiPon process.

Stressed and relaxed state bead images were subsequently analyzed using a previously established 2D TFM  workflow 39 .  Displacement  fields  were  calculated  using  Free  Form  DeformaPon  (FFD)  image registraPon of bead posiPons between the two states 40 (Fig. 1c, top row). TracPon fields were derived from  the  displacement  fields  using  a  Tikhonov-regularized  Fourier  transform  tracPon  cytometry algorithm  (Fig.1c,  top  row) 21,39 .  To  calculate  the  FRET  efficiency  of  the  vinculin  tension  probes, fluorescence  lifePmes  were  analyzed  using  the  phasor  approach 41-45 .  Phasor-transformaPon  of  the donor-specific  FLIM  data  can  be  used  to  directly  retrieve  FRET  efficiencies  from  a  so-called  FRET trajectory  line.  This  line  extends  from  the  average  phasor  posiPon  of  the  donor  in  the  absence  of acceptor (0% FRET), calculated from Clover-only transfected cells, to the posiPon of background signal (100% FRET), obtained from non-transfected cells (Fig. 1c, middle row). The FRET efficiency of VinTSexpressing cells was then calculated from the posiPon of the acquired photons on the FRET trajectory line. Since the calibraPon of the VinTS, which converts FRET efficiency into tensile force, was done in silico 29 , we chose to report FRET efficiencies without converPng them to absolute force values. TFM, FRET efficiencies and acceptor intensity data were integrated using a custom MATLAB code. A binary mask created by IlasPk 46 (Fig. 1c, boRom row) was used to segment the FAs, allowing each FA to be assigned a tracPon, FRET and intensity informaPon, together with morphological features such as FA area and orientaPon.

Correla=ng vinculin tension to trac=on at FA level reveals cell-to-cell heterogeneity

At cell level, both cell-averaged tracPons and vinculin tension increase with substrate sPffness (Figs. 2c and  f),  suggesPng  a  posiPve  correlaPon  between  tracPon  and  vinculin  tension.  However,  neither showed a clear  correlaPon  with  FA  morphology.  We  next  examined  whether  tracPons  and  vinculin tension are correlated at individual FA level (Fig. 4).

Similar  to  the  previous  secPon,  tracPons  and  FRET  efficiency  were  averaged  for  each  FA.  While  no correlaPon was observed when the data from all cells was pooled (Supplementary Fig. 2a), cell-specific analysis revealed disPnct tracPon-vinculin tension correlaPons across individual cells (representaPve cells  in  Fig.  4a-c  and  corresponding  correlaPons  Fig.  4d-f).  To  classify  these  correlaPons,  cells  were grouped as directly correlated (negaPve slope smaller than   -0.1 in FRET versus tracPon graphs; noPng that an inverse correlaPon with FRET efficiency indicates a direct correlaPon with vinculin tension), inversely  correlated  (posiPve  slope  larger  than  0.1),  or  uncorrelated  (slope  between   -0.1  and  0.1; Supplementary Fig. 2b). TracPons and FRET values were normalized to their maximum per cell , confining slope values within   -1 to 1. Among the cells analyzed on the soQ gel, 39% exhibited a direct correlaPon, where  higher  tracPon  coincided  with  higher  vinculin  tension  (and  therefore  lower  FRET  efficiency, Supplementary Fig. 2c). Only 18% of the cells displayed an inverse correlaPon, where larger tracPons corresponded to lower vinculin  tension  across  individual  FAs.  Notably,  43%  of  the  cells  showed  no correlaPon between mean tracPon and vinculin tension. To understand the effect of sPffness on the different correlaPon types, the analysis was repeated for cells seeded on the sPff (13 kPa) substrate. We observed an increase in both directly correlated cells (46%) and uncorrelated cells (51%), and a sharp decrease in indirectly correlated cells, with a rare occurrence of of 2%, indicaPng that the correlaPon between tracPon and vinculin tension at individual FA level is indeed affected by substrate sPffness (Supplementary Fig. 2d).

Previous studies have shown a spaPal distribuPon of vinculin tension along FAs 9,24,29,30 . To explore how these changes relate to tracPons and vinculin density, we analyzed all three variables at the sub-FA level. To account for variability between FAs and cells, each variable was normalized to its maximum and minimum  value  within  each  FA  (Supplementary  Fig.  3). Despite differences in the correlaPon paRerns at the FA level, the spaPal profiles of vinculin density, tracPons  and  vinculin  tension  within  individual  FAs  exhibited  a  consistent  polarity.  Vinculin  density peaked at the center of FAs, with fluorescence intensity significantly higher in this region compared to the proximal and distal ends (Fig. 5b and  e). TracPons and vinculin tension were both significantly higher at  the  distal  Pp,  the  region  farthest  away  from  the  cell  center  (Figs.  5c-g),  suggesPng  a  conserved relaPonship between these two variables at the sub-FA level. Discussion

This work introduces an integrated approach for invesPgaPng FA mechanics, combining simultaneous measurements  of  molecular  tension  and  cellular  tracPons.  By  improving  experimental  sample preparaPon,  streamlining  data  acquisiPon,  and  developing  custom  soQware,  we  achieved  a  highly detailed analysis of FA forces. These methodological advancements allowed us to explore the spaPal coordinaPon of cellular tracPon and vinculin tension, revealing new insights into the adaptability of FAs in force regulaPon and mechanotransducPon.

Fluorescence imaging of cells expressing the VinTS enabled us to examine how FA morphology and vinculin  content  influence  cellular  tracPons  and  molecular  tension.  Smaller  FAs  exhibited  a  broad spectrum of vinculin tension, ranging from very low to very high, while larger FAs were predominantly associated with moderate vinculin tension. This observaPon aligns with earlier findings that smaller FAs experience higher molecular tension, which decreases to average levels as FAs mature 25 . FAs with very low vinculin tension, on the other hand, were mainly located at the cell center, where FAs are typically smaller and subject to compression forces 28 . InteresPngly, vinculin tension was inversely correlated with vinculin density, suggesPng that higher vinculin packing reduces the tension per vinculin molecule. A weaker but similar trend was observed for total vinculin content, indicaPng that FAs may distribute larger forces across more vinculin molecules as the content increases. To support this idea, we found that larger FAs and those with higher vinculin content and density were associated with higher tracPons. While similar correlaPons between tracPons and FA properPes such as size and intensity have been previously reported 47-49 , this correlaPon was only valid for nascent and growing FAs at the cell's leading edge, not for trailing or disassembling FAs 50 . Moreover, according to Zhou et al., FA properPes like area or  vinculin  content  are  insufficient  to  predict  tracPons  and  tension  without  considering  the  FA's maturaPon history 51 . Our findings align with the conclusions of these studies: small FAs with low vinculin content exhibited significant variability in tracPons and vinculin tension, reinforcing the importance of FA maturaPon and context. Unfortunately, due to the heterogeneous shape of the fibroblasts in this study, it was not possible to differenPate between leading and trailing FAs. Future studies using Pmelapse  imaging  combined  with  TFM-FRET  approach  could  provide  clearer  insights  into  how  vinculin tension and tracPons change throughout the FA lifecycle.

Considering  the  roles  of  actomyosin  contracPlity  and  FA  properPes  in  regulaPng  tracPons 52-55 and vinculin recruitment 13,17,56,57 , we invesPgated whether a relaPonship exists between vinculin tension and tracPon at the FA level. While no correlaPon between FA-averaged vinculin tension and tracPon was observed when cells were pooled, analyzing individual cells revealed significant cell-to-cell variability. Nearly half of the cells showed a direct correlaPon, where higher FA vinculin tension corresponded to higher  tracPons.  A  smaller  subset,  however,  exhibited  an  inverse  relaPonship,  where  decreased  FA tension corresponded to elevated tracPons. This variability likely reflects differences in the way cells interact  with  the  ECM.  We  propose  that  highly  engaged  cells,  such  as  those  acPvely  spreading  or migraPng,  exhibit  a  direct  correlaPon  between  FA  tension  and  tracPon.  In  contrast,  cells  with deterioraPng  cell-ECM  interacPons,  such  as  those  retracPng  or  shrinking,  may  display  an  inverse correlaPon.  In  these  cases,  tension  is  released  from  peripheral  FAs  while  central  FAs  remain  under tension. This observaPon aligns with prior studies indicaPng that peripheral FAs are more dynamic and prone  to  disassembly  compared  to  central  FAs 27 .  RetracPng  FAs,  which  are  linked  to  concentrated tracPons before disassembly 8,50 , are known to experience lower vinculin tension 25 . Notably, a significant porPon of cells exhibited no clear correlaPon between FA tension and tracPon. This may suggest that these cells were in a stable, non-migratory state, with with less dynamic cell-ECM interacPons during imaging or that their FAs were in mixed funcPonal phases, with some leading FAs promoPng spreading and others trailing during retracPon. These observaPons emphasize the complexity of FA dynamics, where both direct and inverse correlaPons emerge based on the state of the cell and FA behavior. Combining our TFM-FRET approach alongside tools to modulate acto-myosin acPvity, such as ROCK Inhibitor  Y27632  or  MLCP  inhibitor  Calyculin  A,  combined  with  Pme-lapse  imaging,  could  provide deeper insights into these dynamic relaPonships.

Recognizing cell-to-cell variability in the relaPon between vinculin tension and tracPon, we invesPgated whether these two exhibit disPnct correlaPons at sub-FA level. In agreement with earlier works 9,24,29,30 , sub-FA spaPal analysis revealed disPnct paRerns of vinculin density, tracPon, and tension along the FA length,  reflecPng  a  mechanism  for  force  transmission  that  is  independent  of  cellular  state.  In  the proximal half of the FAs, closer to the cell center, vinculin density gradually increased, driving an increase in tracPon despite the low and stable molecular tension. In contrast, the distal half, closer to the cell periphery, showed a decreasing vinculin density alongside an increasing vinculin tension, suggesPng that higher vinculin tension compensates for reduced vinculin density to sustain force transmission. These results suggest a spaPal coordinaPon between vinculin density and tension to regulate tracPons, highlighPng the adaptability of FAs in force generaPon and mechanotransducPon. Unexpectedly, unlike the  FA-level  results,  vinculin  density  and  tension  were  not  anPcorrelated  at  sub-FA  level.  Previous studies using the vinculin conformaPon sensor (VinCS), have shown that vinculin adopts an open, acPve conformaPon more frequently at the proximal end of FAs 6,10 . This suggests that vinculin density alone may  not  regulate  tension;  instead,  the  amount  of  acPvated  vinculin,  capable  of  engaging  in  force transmission, could be the key factor. We hypothesize that the molecular tension is lower at the proximal end, where acPvated vinculin is more abundant, and higher at the distal end, where vinculin acPvaPon decreases. Overall, these results indicate that vinculin tension is regulated at two levels: vinculin density determines overall tension at the FA level, while vinculin acPvaPon controls tension distribuPon at the sub-FA level. To explore this further, future studies could combine TFM-FRET with VinCS or semi-digital vinculin  tension  probes 31,58,59 to  invesPgate  how  vinculin  acPvaPon  state  and  its  spaPal  distribuPon shape the relaPonship between vinculin tension and tracPons.

The substanPal scaRer in the data across all experiments in this study suggests that a linear relaPonship between  mean  tracPon  and  vinculin  tension  may  not  be  feasible.  TracPons  likely  depend  on  the combined effect  of  vinculin  tension,  density  and  acPvaPon,  rather  than  vinculin  tension  alone.  For instance, low vinculin tension paired with high acPve vinculin density could generate tracPons similar to high tension with low acPve vinculin density. AddiPonally, vinculin tension and tracPons may not be temporally synchronized. Vinculin tension fluctuates on short Pmescales 24 ,  while  tracPons, as largescale outputs, take longer to adjust. Since stress-state bead images for TFM were acquired before FLIM imaging, vinculin tension changes during FLIM acquisiPon might not be reflected in the tracPon data. Finally, while vinculin plays a key role in FA stability and maturaPon, it is dispensable for FA formaPon and tracPon generaPon 13,60 .  Exploring other FA proteins, such as talin or integrin, may yield stronger correlaPons with tracPon forces in future studies.

In  conclusion,  this  study  presents  a  novel  approach  for  simultaneously  measuring  tracPons  and molecular  tension  within  FAs,  enabling  new  insights  into  their  mechanics.  We  showed  that  the relaPonship between vinculin tension and tracPon depends on cellular states and FA context. Sub-FA analysis revealed a spaPal coordinaPon of vinculin density and tension, allowing FAs to balance forces and sustain effecPve tracPon. metadata.preprint_title: Linking Molecular Tension and Cellular Tractions: A Multiscale Approach to Focal Adhesion Mechanics

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Linking Molecular Tension and Cellular Tractions: A Multiscale Approach to Focal Adhesion Mechanics
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Aytekin, S.; Kimps, L.; Coucke, Q.; Linhares, D.; Deodhar, S.; Cardinaels, R.; Condor, M.; Barrasa-Fano, J.; Van Oosterwyck, H.; Rocha, S.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Focal adhesions (FAs) are mechanosensitive structures that mediate force transmission between cells and the extracellular matrix. While Traction Force Microscopy (TFM) quantifies cellular tractions exerted on deformable substrates, Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET)-based tension probes, such as Vinculin Tension Sensors (VinTS), measure molecular-scale forces within FA proteins. Despite their potential synergy, these methods have rarely been combined to explore the interplay between molecular tension and cellular tractions. Here, we introduce a framework integrating TFM and VinTS to investigate FA mechanics across scales. At cell level, tractions and vinculin tension increased with substrate stiffness. At FA level, vinculin tension correlated with vinculin density, while tractions scaled with FA area, total vinculin content and vinculin density. Direct comparison of tractions to tension revealed a complex, heterogenous relationship between these forces, possibly linked to diverse cell and FA maturation states. Sub-FA analysis revealed conserved spatial patterns, with tension and traction increasing towards the cell periphery. This multiscale approach provides insights into the multiscale dynamics of FA mechanotransduction, bridging the gap between molecular forces and cellular mechanics.j
